summary: The author achieved 6th place in a math misconception classification competition by fine-tuning Qwen family LLMs (8B and 14B) using Q-LoRA. Key strategies included deduplicating training data, pseudo-labeling duplicates, generating synthetic data for rare labels via ChatGPT 4o, and applying targeted text augmentations. The final submission relied on an ensemble of multiple Qwen models, where even lower-performing individual models contributed positively to the collective score.
extensive_summary:
## Problem Formulation & Data Handling
The competition required predicting answer correctness, explanation correctness/misconception status, and specific misconception types from student math responses. The author reframed the task as a 37-class misconception classification problem by stripping `True_`/`False_` prefixes from the original labels. A strict prompt template was constructed, explicitly appending a task instruction line ("Task: Classify the misconception in the explanation.") which was found to consistently improve both cross-validation and leaderboard scores.

## Data Processing & Augmentation
Initial data cleaning involved dropping exact duplicates (same answer and explanation but conflicting labels). In later stages, these duplicates were pseudo-labeled using an ensemble of models and reintroduced to the training set. To address class imbalance, the author generated ~50 targeted augmentations per category (punctuation replacement, word-to-number conversion, typo injection) based on fuzzy-searched near-duplicates. For the 10 rarest categories, synthetic data was generated using ChatGPT 4o, yielding 100-120 samples per class. Sequences were dynamically padded to a maximum length of 160 tokens to optimize training and inference speed.

## Model Training & Architecture
The core approach leveraged Q-LoRA fine-tuning (rank=128, alpha=32-128) on Qwen family models, specifically `Qwen3-Embedding-8B`, `Qwen3 14B`, and `Qwen2.5 14B IT`. The `sequence classification` head on `Qwen3-Embedding-8B` emerged as the strongest single model. Training was conducted on A5000/A100 GPUs via Jarvis Labs. The author systematically tested various data combinations (deduplicated, augmented, pseudo-labeled, synthetic) across different model sizes and random seeds.

## Ensembling & Final Submission
The final solution utilized an ensemble method adapted from a community notebook. The ensemble of the first four models crossed 0.95 on the public LB and was subsequently used to pseudo-label the previously dropped duplicates. Adding a model trained on augmented data, despite its weaker individual performance, consistently boosted the ensemble score. The final submission combined six models, including a Qwen3 14B run with a different random seed that individually scored poorly but provided crucial diversity to the ensemble, ultimately securing the 6th place rank.
competition_flow: Raw student math Q&A data was cleaned and deduplicated, then enriched with targeted augmentations and ChatGPT-generated synthetic samples for rare classes, before being formatted into a fixed prompt template and fine-tuned via Q-LoRA on Qwen LLMs, with final predictions aggregated through a multi-model ensemble.
data_processing: Dropped exact duplicates (same answer/explanation, different label)
Pseudo-labeled duplicates and added them back to training data
Generated ~50 targeted augmentations per category (punctuation replacement, word-to-number conversion, typo injection) based on fuzzy-searched near-duplicates
Generated 100-120 synthetic samples per category for the 10 rarest classes using ChatGPT 4o
Formatted inputs into a fixed prompt template with an explicit task instruction line
Dynamically padded sequences to max length 160
ensembling: The final submission combined six Qwen models via a community-sourced ensemble method, where even lower-performing individual models contributed positively to the collective score, and the ensemble outputs were used to pseudo-label previously dropped duplicate samples.
insights: Appending an explicit task instruction line to the prompt consistently improved both CV and LB scores.
Models with weaker individual scores can still significantly boost ensemble performance if they provide complementary diversity.
Targeted augmentations based on fuzzy-searched near-duplicates are more effective than blindly generating large volumes of synthetic data.
Fine-tuning with a different random seed can yield a model that performs poorly alone but adds crucial value to an ensemble.
critical_findings: Adding a model trained on augmented data consistently improved the ensemble score despite its poor individual leaderboard performance.
Dropping duplicates initially was necessary, but pseudo-labeling and reintroducing them later provided a significant score boost.
Creating ~10k augmented samples based on multiple patterns yielded no public LB improvement, highlighting the importance of targeted over brute-force augmentation.
what_did_not_work: Using multiple pre-trained models without fine-tuning.
Generating ~10k augmented samples across multiple patterns without targeted selection.
Using all original 65 labels instead of the consolidated 37.
A 2-stage classification approach (correctness then misconception type).
Text generation and textual entailment prompting strategies.
Using an MoE-style NN for the classification head instead of LoRA adapters.
